children.name: Aegotheles
children.description: Small, nocturnal birds that resemble a cross between an owl and a nightjar, with a finely patterned plumage, a slightly hooked bill and a soft, fluttering flight. They are endemic to Australasia and are among the most mysterious of all bird families.

children.name: Aegotheles cristatus
children.description: The most common and widespread owlet-nightjar, found across mainland Australia and Tasmania. It is a small, grey-brown bird measuring about 22 centimetres, with a short tail and a distinctive pale collar. Despite its name, it is more closely related to swifts and hummingbirds than to true nightjars.

children.name: Aegotheles albertisi
children.description: A montane species found in the highland forests of New Guinea at elevations between 1,000 and 3,200 metres. Its plumage is a rich rufous-brown with fine black vermiculations and a mottled breast, providing excellent camouflage against moss-covered branches. It is known for its penetrating, chattering call at dusk.

children.name: Aegotheles crinifrons
children.description: Endemic to the islands of Halmahera and Bacan in the North Maluku region of Indonesia, this species inhabits primary lowland and hill forests. It is larger than other owlet-nightjars with a shaggy, elongated crown and nape feathers. Very little is known about its ecology and behaviour in the wild.
